observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
a. Create a new Inventory Committee comprised of members who are
capable and with sufficient time to conduct the annual physical inventory
count. Further, direct the Inventory Committee to perform the complete
physical count of PPE in accordance with the procedures stated in the
“One Time Cleansing of PPE”. This includes the initial preparation of PIP
to set out the process, procedures and time frame of physical count.
Likewise, prepare the RPCPPE;
b. Require the GSO to maintain the PPE Property Cards;
c. Require the Municipal Accountant the PPE Subsidiary Ledger Cards; and
d. Require both GSO and Municipal Accountant to conduct reconciliation of
their property records and avail the one-time cleansing of PPE account
balances, strictly in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006,
particularly on the disposition and derecognition of non-existing/missing
PPE and adjust the books accordingly, in order to come up with reliable
PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, conditions and
accountability.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Direct officers and employees with unliquidated CA to immediately submit
their liquidation papers, otherwise, may constitute to a valid case for the
withholding of salary of those officers and employees who were unable to
comply with the requirement;
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to closely monitor the liquidation of
outstanding CA and issue on a regular basis demand letter to submit
liquidation reports immediately;
c. Fast track the recovery of the documents relevant to the long outstanding CA
and conduct verification, analysis and validation of them to determine their
settlement/collectability and pursue collection, refund or liquidation, if
warranted;
d. Refrain from granting Clearance from Money and Accountability to those
retiring/transferring employees with unliquidated CA, unless the same is
settled; and
e. Stop the practice of granting additional CA to those who have not submitted
the supporting documents of their Liquidation Reports and those who have
unliquidated CA.

We recommended that Management:
a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to properly account the acquisition and
issuance of the supplies and materials inventory using the Perpetual
Inventory System and make the necessary adjustments to reflect the
correct balance of the affected inventory and expense accounts;
b. Require the GSO and Municipal Accountant to maintain SCs and SLCs per
stock number, respectively, and regularly reconcile the balances of
inventory based on their respective records;
c. Require the newly hired GSO or any designated Property custodian to
prepare the RIS upon issuances of inventory and weekly consolidate the
same in SSMI. The SSMI and the original copies of RIS must be promptly
submitted to the Municipal Accountant to record the expense portion of
inventory issued; and
d. Create a functional Inventory Committee whose members are capable and
with available time to perform the physical count. Require the Committee
also to promptly prepare and submit the RPCI to the Municipal
Accountant for recording and reconciliation purposes, and to COA for
audit verification.

We recommended that Management requires the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Make immediate adjustment of financial records by recognizing the
completed Infrastructure Assets in the financial statements. A journal entry
must be made by debiting the appropriate Asset accounts and crediting the
CIP accounts (Infrastructure Assets/Building and Other Structures) in the
total amount of ₱30,566,535.62;
b. Implement stricter internal controls over the recording and classification
of infrastructure assets. This may involve establishing clear procedures for
asset verification, project completion documentation, timely and proper
recording of transactions, including the Subsidiary Ledgers, pursuant to the
Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs. A checklist system for project
completion could be particularly helpful; and
c. Reconciliation must be made on the beginning balances of CIP Accounts
and detailed information must be provided for each CIP items/projects.
Proper disclosure of accounting errors must be included in the Notes to the
Financial Statements, explaining the nature of the error, the amount of the
correction, and the impact on the financial statements. Further, strictly
comply with the requirements under IPSAS 1.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to make the
necessary adjusting entries to correct the noted misstatement. Also, ensure that
the Municipality is compliant with the accounting guidelines set out for the
grants received from the national government agencies in subsequent
transactions.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management require the Municipal
Accountant to review or trace the cause of the negative balances posted in the
ledgers of the Inter-Agency Payable accounts, prepare the necessary adjusting
entries and update the affected ledger accounts in order to present the correct
amount in the Financial Statements and complete the said reconciliation before
the end of CY 2025.

We recommended that Management require the Collecting Clerks in the MTO
to strictly comply with the timely remittance of their collections to the Municipal
Treasurer/Liquidating Officer as provided in Section 26 of COA Circular No. 92-
382 and Section 29 Volume I of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs. Likewise, the
Collecting Clerks exercise care and diligence in the preparation of RCD and
recording of transactions in the cashbook to avoid complexity and discrepancies
with other records.

We recommended that Management:
a. Avoid the practice of charging expressly prohibited expense items
enumerated in Section 3.2.5 of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1, dated
November 4, 2020 to 20 percent DF so that development programs and
projects envisioned by the government are properly implemented; and
b. Ensure that development projects included under the 20 percent DF are those
that are necessary, appropriate, or incidental to efficient and effective local
governance, and those which are essential to promotion of the general welfare
of the people.

We recommended that Management:
a. Facilitate the reversion to the GF of the unexpended balances from DRRMF-
Capital Outlay transferred to the STF amounting in total to ₱8,733.40 and
ensure that only the unexpended balances of QRF and MF-MOOE are
transferred to the STF while the unexpended balance of MF-Capital Outlay be
made continuing in the GF books;
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal
Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (MDRRMO) to revert the
CY 2018 STF balance of ₱147,113.85 that have remained unutilized for more
than five years to the GF; and
c. Ensure that any STF-DRRMF balance remaining unutilized after five years
are reverted to the GF for allocation to other social services subject to the
approval by the Local Sanggunian.

We recommended that Management require the concerned Municipal Officials
and Employees to devise measures and exert more effort to expedite the
preparation and submission of the accounts and financial reports, including the
supporting documents and schedules, within the prescribed period. We request
that such activities be given utmost priority for timely and better fiscal reporting.
Moreover, submit immediately the lacking financial transaction documents to
COA.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with the mandatory
submission of contracts, POs and their supporting documents to the Office of the
Auditor within five days after perfection for a timely and complete conduct of
auditorial and technical review.

Further, direct the personnel responsible for accepting deliveries to notify
COA of the time and date of the scheduled deliveries, furnish copies of AIRs within
24 hours upon acceptance and submit report on deliveries received and issued as of
year-end.

We recommended that Management require the LSB to strictly comply with the
provisions of DepEd, DBM and DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017 to ensure that prescribed
planning, budgeting and reporting tools are effectively utilized and the timelines
thereof are strictly observed.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the concerned LGU officials and employees to submit the lacking
documents of the aforementioned financial transactions particularly the
Travel Order duly approved by the Municipal Mayor and in original copy;
and
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that claims for the payment of
travelling expenses or cash advances must be supported with complete
documentation prior facilitating payment of transactions. A Certification
that the previous cash advance has been liquidated and accounted for in the
books must also be issued before granting the succeeding cash advances.

We recommended that Management:
a. Direct the GFPS and GAD key personnel to undergo capacity building
programs and training on GAD to enhance their knowledge and build their
competencies on GAD-related laws and international commitments, gender
mainstreaming, gender analysis, gender-responsive planning and
budgeting, and GAD-related tools like HGDG on the attributed budget to
evaluate gender responsiveness of PPAs in the GPB; and
b. Ensure that the PPAs charged to GAD fund are only those that would
address the identified gender issues in the locality. The gender issues must
be specific and supported by evidence such as sex-disaggregated data and
gender analysis results.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to update the subsidiary ledger for accounts Due to NGAs and Due to LGUs to support the balance presented in the financial statements.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare and record the necessary adjusting journal entries to accurately reclassify these accounts to the appropriate semi-expendable property accounts or expense accounts if issued to end-users, in adherence to COA Circular 2024-006 dated March 14, 2024,

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to make the necessary adjusting journal entries to reclassify the completed projects recorded under the Construction in Progress (CIP) accounts to the appropriate Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE) accounts. Additionally, the accountant will ensure that depreciation is recognized from the date the assets became available for use, in accordance with applicable accounting standards.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to draw the disbursement voucher to revert back to the General Fund the unutilized DRRRM funds from CY 2019 and earlier in the special trust fund and prepare the necessary accounting entry to record the same.
We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the adjusting journal entries to reflect the correct balance of account Due to NGAs.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Review and reconcile the credit balances on the Advances to Officers and Employees account and prepare the necessary adjusting journal entry to correct the balances; and
Reclassify non-travel-related transactions that were erroneously recorded under the Advances to Officers and Employees account to the appropriate accounts, to ensure proper presentation and classification of accounts in the financial statements.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation and the Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare adjusting journal entries to recognize all valid and identified reconciling items, duly supported with appropriate documents, that require adjustment and correction.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the journal entries to reflect the liquidation of confidential funds already submitted and duly received by the COA-ICFAU in accordance with Section 6.2.2.4 of DBM-DILG-DND-GCG-COA Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to apply adjusting journal entries to reclassify the erroneously recorded expenses as capital assets and recognize the corresponding depreciation to accurately reflect the assets' value over their useful life, in compliance with International Public Sector Accounting Standard (IPSAS) 17 – Property, Plant, and Equipment.
We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to draw the adjusting journal entry to correct the balance of Due to PhilHealth account.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Direct the Municipal Accountant and the MDRRMO to establish the unexpended/unobligated balance for the QRF and MOOE-DRRMF from CYs 2020-2024;
Require the Municipal Accountant to draw the disbursement voucher to transfer the unexpended/unobligated balance to the special trust fund and prepare the necessary accounting entry to record the same; and
Require the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that items of appropriations for LDDRMF coincide with the LDRRMFIP and ensure appropriate charging of disbursements.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Apply adjusting entries to properly reclassify any unclosed balances related to the above-mentioned liability transactions to their appropriate accounts; and
Exercise due diligence in the recording and classification of transactions in its appropriate accounts. Moving forward, consistently refer to COA Circular 2015-009 whenever there is uncertainty regarding the appropriate account classification for a transaction.

We recommended and Management agreed to discontinue this practice and ensure that checks are signed only after all necessary details have been provided, duly supported by the corresponding disbursement vouchers and their complete supporting documents. This process will help mitigate the risk of unauthorized disbursements and potential misuse of funds.

We recommended and Management agreed to strictly and consistently enforce existing documentation policies and procedures, particularly the implementation of standardized prenumbering system for all disbursement vouchers to enhance accountability and compliance with sound internal control practices.

We recommended and Management agreed to require all Department Heads to stop claiming overtime pay in strict compliance with CSC-DBM Joint Circular No. 2, s. 2015, dated November 25, 2015, which governs the payment of authorized overtime services for government personnel.

We recommended and Management agreed to immediately discontinue the practice of charging payments for COS and JO workers under Personnel Services (PS) and instead classify these under Other Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses account.

We recommended and Management agreed to expedite the adoption of the Electronic New Government Accounting System to enhance efficiency and expedite the preparation of financial statements, thereby ensuring timely submission and compliance with reporting requirements.

We recommended that Management discontinue the grant of Representation and Transportation Allowance (RATA) to the Acting Municipal Treasurer and instead compensate her with honoraria in compliance with DBM Local Budget Circular No. 62, issued on July 29, 1996. Furthermore, we recommended that her eligibility to claim reimbursements for daily travel expenses incurred during official duties be duly recognized and maintained.

We recommended and Management agreed that, through the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Office (MSWDO), ensure the prompt submission of the Gender and Development (GAD) Accomplishment Report to ensure compliance with Section C.9 of Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01 issued by PCW, DILG, DBM, and NEDA. Timely submission of this report will facilitate the audit and verification of the judicious utilization of GAD funds and the effectiveness of gender-responsive programs.

We recommended and Management agreed to strictly comply with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2012-002 by ensuring the timely preparation and submission of the Report on Sources and Utilization of the LDRRM Funds to the Auditor within the prescribed deadline.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to:
Prioritize the return of the unused balances of fund transfers from CY 2012-2024 to source agencies;
Prepare the Fund Utilization Reports (FURs) of the projects already implemented and submit to this Office; and
Record the liquidation in the accounting books.

We recommended and Management agreed to prioritize and expedite the full implementation of e-NGAS to optimize operational efficiency, improve accuracy in financial reporting, and streamline accounting processes across all transactions. This initiative is critical to addressing persistent delays in the submission of financial reports and ensuring timely compliance with reporting requirements.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations, with modification, and Management agreed to:
Realign continuing appropriations from projects that are no longer feasible to other eligible projects that are ready for implementation; and
Prioritize the implementation of projects to achieve essential socio-economic benefits that effectively meet the needs and expectations of constituents.

We recommended and Management agreed to strictly comply with Section 124 of the Manual on New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs) by conducting regular physical counts, preparing the required reports, and ensuring their timely submission to the Auditor within the prescribed deadline.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to comply with the prescribed timeline for BRS preparation as outlined in COA Circular No. 96-011 to ensure timely submission and facilitate accurate financial monitoring and reporting.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Require the Municipal Accountant to send demand letters to the concerned officials and persons and enforce the immediate liquidation/ refund of their unliquidated cash advances; and
Strictly enforce the existing rules and regulations on the timely liquidation of cash advances, particularly those provided under COA Circular No. 2012-001.
Stop the practice of granting additional cash advances to those who have not submitted the supporting documents of their Liquidation Reports and those who have unliquidated CA.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management submit the necessary supporting documents to establish the propriety of the distribution of the relief goods such as inventory of the supplies at year-end and distribution list signed by the recipients.
Henceforth, require the accounting department to follow the relevant rules and regulations in recording the procurement of supplies for distribution.

We recommended that Management immediately submit to this office, the detailed distribution lists, duly signed and acknowledge received by the beneficiaries, to account for the complete delivery of relief goods purchased.
We recommended that Management, in coordination with offices concerned, revisit COA Circular No. 2020-006 and comply with all the guidelines and procedures provided therein.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the year-end financial statements and schedules on or before February 14 of each year in compliance with COA Circular 2010-001.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit the approved suspension order and, henceforth, require the preparation of detailed engineering prior to the procurement of infrastructure projects to ensure completion as scheduled.
We recommended that Management require immediate full liquidation of the subject cash advance, otherwise, withhold the accountable officer’s salary until the cash advance is fully liquidated.

We recommended Management to ask a formal approval from the Bureau of Local Government Finance (BLGF) on the use of above bank accounts based on the provisions in DOF Circular 001-2005 and transfer immediately any amount in excess of the authorized cash balance to any GFIs in Section 5.2.
We recommended that Management strictly ensure that none of the employee’s take home pay is below the ₱5,000.00 threshold as prescribed.

Management should as well assess carefully the effects of additional loan applications on the employee’s net take home pay considering the existing deductions on their salaries and wages before issuing certifications and clearances as a lending requirement.
We recommended the Management to strictly impose the withholding of salary on employees with cash advances which are not liquidated within the prescribed period.

We recommended that Management submit the necessary supporting documents to establish the propriety of the distribution of the relief goods, medicines and medical and laboratory supplies such as inventory of the supplies at year-end and distribution list signed by the recipients.
Henceforth, require the accounting department to follow the relevant rules and regulations in recording the procurement of supplies for distribution.

Also, a Report of Fuel Consumption shall be prepared and submitted on a monthly basis by the Agency personnel concerned in compliance with COA Circular No. 77-61 to determine the reasonableness of the Fuel, Oil and Lubricants Expenses.

We recommended that Management avail the procedures to write off these receivables and instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the documents required per Item 8.3 of COA Circular No. 2016-005 in order to facilitate submission of request for write-off of dormant accounts which are no longer recoverable from deceased LGU official.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reclassify as non-current assets, the portion of the receivables which do not conform to the criteria provided for in paragraph 76 of the IPSAS in order to present fairly the financial statements of the municipality.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to trace the long outstanding balance of ₱6,287,504.28, that also includes ₱2,887,337.32 errors and misclassifications as well as accounting entries pertaining to the ₱36,687.19 accounts to ascertain the cause of the abnormal balances and to effect the necessary adjustments in order to present a reliable balance of the Due to LGUs account.

Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain the required Abstract of Real Property Collections in order to monitor and control the release of shares of Real Property and Community Taxes due the barangays.

Require the Municipal Treasurer to release and or remit in full the amount of ₱1,094,297.45 due the different barangays so that the same could be utilized in implementing the development projects of their respective areas.

We recommended that Management abide by the provisions of COA Circular No. 94-013 through the immediate utilization of the funds received, timely and proper liquidation of all implemented projects and remittance of the remaining balances thereof to source agency of the excess funds.

We recommended that Management exhaust all available remedies provided by law and regulations in enforcing the immediate settlement of the audit disallowances in accordance with Section 7.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.

We recommended that Management submit the complete set of approved plans and drawings including the detailed breakdown of the approved budget for the contract, unit cost analysis, program of work and the detailed engineering design for the project.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management:
Reconstitute the Inventory Committee and require them to conduct physical count of all the Municipality’s properties annually and prepare and submit the RPCPPE;
Facilitate the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances through the implementation of COA Circular No. 2020-006; and
Require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain property records for all PPE and undertake the required reconciliation of the Property and Accounting records thereafter.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE):
Direct the Municipal Accountant to exert more efforts in retrieving the previous years’ records to verify the balances of the Due to NGAs account;
Require the Municipal Accountant to continue the preparation and maintenance of SLs and records of the Agency accounts duly supported with complete documents to substantiate the balances in the FS and
Direct the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer, formerly assigned Municipal Accountant, to retrieve her files and help the current Municipal Accountant in verifying the balances of the aforementioned account.

We recommended that the LCE:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to give preferential attention in the preparation and submission of the required BRS, maintain SLs per bank accounts, review the records relevant to the Cash in Bank account and effect necessary adjustments to correct the amount presented in the FS and other documents;
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to review the records of her cashbooks, reconcile them with the bank statements and the SLs of the Municipal accountant and effect necessary adjustments to reflect the correct amount; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to determine the source of the four bank accounts amounting to ₱969,817.15 transferred from the TF to GF. These balances must be monitored and ensured that these will not be used for general purposes until the source is determined.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct verification, analysis and validation of these receivable balances, to determine their collectability and pursue collection, refund or liquidation thereof, if warranted. We also recommended that the LCE file the request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts which could no longer be collected to COA, duly supported with the required documents.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Conduct an in-depth verification of the Due to BIR, GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth accounts, perform reconciliation between the SLs and the Schedules of Remittances and remit immediately the withheld funds due to BIR, GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth, if warranted;
Evaluate the current system of withholding and remitting taxes, premiums, contributions and other deductions to prevent under or over remittance thereof; and
Prepare the necessary adjusting entries to present the correct account balance of the Due to Pag-IBIG account and the corresponding Agricultural and Marine Supplies for Distribution and Due to PhilHealth accounts.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management require the Municipal Accountant to review or trace the cause of the negative balances posted in the ledgers of the Inter-Agency Payable accounts, prepare the necessary adjusting entries and update the affected ledger accounts in order to present the correct amount in the Financial Statements and complete the said reconciliation before the end of CY 2024.

We recommended that Management prioritize securing the appropriate supporting documents for the purchased lots as enumerated in COA Circular No. 2012-001, including Deed of Sale, together with the TCT, TD, RPT Certificate, Deed of Extra Judicial Settlement (for the undivided lot) and other necessary documents, to support the claims of ownership and to safeguard the rights the Municipality, and to submit them to the Office of the Auditor as soon as possible.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit to the Office of the Auditor the DVs and the supporting documents relative to the procurement of lot for water source for review and evaluation.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant strengthen the internal control in the pre-audit of financial transactions to ensure that the supporting documents are complete and properly accomplished before processing the claim or payment of the same.

We recommended that Management strictly follow the provisions of the Manual on Audit for Fuel Consumption of Government Motor Vehicles prescribed under COA Circular No. 77-61, as amended by COA Circular No. 92-61A dated April 2, 1992, such as to:
Require all drivers to accomplish the DTTs with all information required and ensure that they are duly signed by the authorized signatories and passenger/s after each trip and prepare the Monthly Report of Official Travels for each vehicle summarizing all trips from the month;
Ensure that every transaction for fuel be supported by the duly accomplished and approved RIV or equivalent (PR) as the basis of procuring gasoline and oil from the service stations; and
Prepare and submit a monthly fuel consumption report showing among others, the different types of motor vehicles utilized by the Agency during the month, total distance traveled, total fuel used and the normal fuel consumption for each vehicle.

We recommended that Management:
Stop the practice of splitting of contracts and strictly adhere to the provisions of RA No. 9184 in all procurements;
Render an explanation or justification on the resort to alternative methods of procurement instead of competitive bidding in the purchase of various goods and services; and
Direct the Municipal Official/Employees concerned to explain the other deficiencies noted on the particular disbursements and submit the lacking supporting documents for each transaction.

We recommended that Management:
Ensure timely implementation of infrastructure projects thru effective monitoring of the performance of contractors and adopt calibrated measures as provided under GPPB Circular No. 03-2019, particularly when contractors incur negative slippage during the contract duration; and
Require the Municipal Engineer and the contractors to include and specify the materials, labor and costs of project signboards in the Program of Works and Detailed Cost Estimates and submit documents such as photographs of the project signboards to ascertain that relevant information on all infrastructure projects are duly posted, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2013-004.

We recommended that the LCE:
Require the Municipal Accountant to submit immediately the lacking DVs complete with supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor; and
Evaluate the process of preparing the DVs and necessary documents to prevent further occurrence of this deficiency in the succeeding years and be reminded that processing of vouchers must only be undertaken when they were properly approved and with complete documentation.

We recommended that the LCE require the BAC to strictly comply with the mandatory submission of contracts, POs and their supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within five days after perfection for a timely and complete conduct of auditorial and technical review. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the lacking DVs including the aforementioned transactions amounting to ₱5,492,267.07.
We also invited the attention of Management to Section 4.0, the Penalty Clause, of COA Circular No. 2009-001, which states that the inability of the officials and employees concerned to comply with the requirements of the same must be subject to administrative disciplinary action and the transactions covered by the unsubmitted documents may be suspended in audit.

We recommended that the LCE direct the GFPS to:
Ensure that the essential elements in GAD planning and budgeting are in place such as capacity development on gender mainstreaming, gender-responsive planning and budgeting, gender analysis and gender assessment and development and utilization of GAD tools;
Utilize the GAD Fund only for acceptable PPAs that would address the identified gender issues in the locality based on RA No. 9710 Magna Carta for Women, PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-001 and other relevant international commitments; and
Consider attribution of a portion or the whole of the Agency’s major programs to the GAD Budget. As provided in Section 1.2.2.2 of PCW Memorandum Circular No. 2020-05, agencies are advised to use the tool for budget attribution.

We recommended and the Management agreed to prepare the journal entry to recognize the liquidation of the fund transfers to reflect the accurate outstanding balance of the Due from LGUs account in the financial statements. Moreover, support the entries by attaching a copy of the Fund Utilization Reports, duly received by this Office.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Ensure that at the end of each year, the Municipal Accountant transfers all the unexpended/unobligated balance of the QRF and the DRRMF - MOOE to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liability - DRRM”.
Accordingly, make the necessary journal entries to transfer the unexpended QRF and DRRMF-MOOE to the special trust account for 2023:
Require the Municipal Accountant to transfer all unexpended QRF and LDRRMF-MOOE of qualified prior years to the Special Trust Fund under the account Trust Liabilities-DRRM with the following adjusting entry:
Unadjusted reconciling items for Cash in Bank – LCCA - ₱1.618 million
The reconciling items identified in the Bank Reconciliation Statements were not adjusted in the agency’s book of accounts, inconsistent with Item 3.3 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996, thus affecting the reliability of the Cash in Bank – LCCA totaling ₱1,617,749.49 as presented in the financial statement as at December 31, 2023.

We recommended and the Management agreed to prepare adjusting journal entries to recognize all valid and identified reconciling items, duly supported with appropriate documents, that require adjustment and correction.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to course thru the appropriate inventory account, purchases of supplies and materials and recognize the expense only upon issuance and submission of the SSMI by the Municipal Treasurer.

We recommended and the Management agreed to review and analyze the records of the account to ascertain the cause of abnormal or negative balance, reconcile accordingly to arrive at the correct balance, and reclassify to its appropriate account, if warranted.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Correct the understatement by applying necessary adjusting entries;
Proposed Adjusting Journal Entries are as follows:
Compute depreciation in accordance with COA Circular 2015-008.
We recommended and the Management agreed to fast track the implementation of the projects for timely delivery of socio-economic benefits to its constituents.

During the exit conference, management appealed for consideration by the audit team to temporarily retain the time deposit accounts to provide additional local revenues, improve liquidity, and accordingly increase the PS Cap of the LGU. Furthermore, management pointed out that the said time deposit accounts have maturity values of 30 days, implying availability for immediate withdrawal of such funds as the need arises.
Thus, after thorough consideration of Management’s appeal, we recommended that Management hasten the implementation of PPAs and realign the budget of projects that are no longer feasible and cannot be implemented to other development projects for optimal use of funds and ensure timely delivery of services to the people.

Require the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant to properly maintain Stock Cards, Property Cards, SLC and PPELC and conduct periodic reconciliation of property and accounting records to come up with accurate reports.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management direct the:

Municipal Treasurer to regularly update the cash books and reflect the reconciling items per BRS, and close accounts that have become dormant and revert back to the Source Agency or the Bureau of the Treasury, as the case maybe, if the balances represent excess of the implemented PPAs.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management require:
The Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere to the prescribed rules on proper recording of RPT and SET Receivables under Section 248 of RA No. 7160 and Section 20, Volume I of NGAS for LGUs;
The Municipal Treasurer to exert more effort in improving the collection efficiency of the Municipality by posting the Notice of Delinquency in the Payment of RPT at the main entrance of Municipal Hall and other publicly accessible place and conspicuous place in each barangay as required under Section 254 of RA No. 7160 or mailing demand letters to its constituents individually in order to compel them to settle their tax liability; and
The Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain subsidiary ledgers pertaining to RPT and SET Receivables.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management facilitate the request for write-off of the aforementioned dormant accounts duly supported by relevant documents validating the existence of the conditions. A request can be made to balances with available supporting records at present. The Audit Team will decide on the request for authority to write off for amounts not exceeding ₱100,000.00 per AO. Records for amounts exceeding ₱100,000.00 will be forwarded to the Regional Director for review and appropriate action.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that Fund Transfers (FTs) coming from other government agencies be properly recorded in the TF books. A trust liability must be recognized while the specific purpose(s) for which the funds were received is not yet implemented.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to strengthen accounting controls over liabilities by reviewing thoroughly the schedule of payables due to concerned agencies during each month and ensure that remittances are based on the actual payments made, and not on list of checks listed in the Accountant’s Advice. Records with the Treasury Office must be reconciled to identify all cancelled checks for GSIS and Philhealth remittance in the prior years. A correcting entry must be effected to adjust the correct balance for the said accounts.

We recommended that Management require the concerned official to ensure that monthly premium contributions withheld from employed members of the LGU be remitted promptly to authorized government agencies to avoid surcharges and penalties and to ensure that benefit medical packages and social security benefits of its employees will not be affected.

We recommended that Management:
Require the BAC and other officials concerned to submit immediately the required documents as cited in R.A. 3720 as amended by EO No. 175, Executive Order No. 49 and other pertinent laws in the procurement of drugs, medicines. Strict adherence thereto must at all times be observed;
Direct the Municipal Accountant and her staff to ensure that supporting documents are properly and completely filled-out before effecting any payments; and
Ensure compliance to Section 10 of RA No. 9184 which mandates that government procurements shall be done through competitive bidding.  Procurements which can be embraced in one contract should be captured in one procurement plan and should not be split into artificial phases. Management is reminded that the alternative mode of procurement can only be availed of only in highly exceptional cases.

We recommended that Management:
Require the LDRRM Officer to submit to the Office of the Auditor the report on sources and utilization of LDRRMF on or before the 15th day after the end of each month. A Supplemental LDRRMFIP must be prepared if there additional PPAs to be implemented during the year; and
Ensure that only unutilized balance of the QRF and the DRRMF-MOOE is transferred to the special trust fund. All unexpended/unobligated balance for CO must be made continuing in the GF books until the projects funded therefore are completed and any savings must be available for use in the disaster risk reduction and management activities as provided in the LDRRMFIP.

We recommended that Management, through the Legislative and Human Resource Department, enact or approve resolution that would facilitate immediate creation and hiring of two additional positions in the LDRRMO as prescribed under NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC 2014-1.

We recommended that Management require the beneficiary of the fund transfers charged against SEF to submit liquidation reports or documents showing fund utilization to avoid audit disallowance thereof. Disbursements of SEF through issuance of check in the name of a particular personnel of beneficiary schools must now be stopped. Disbursement must undergo the correct process to ensure that funds are utilized for its intended purpose.

We recommended that Management require the LSB to immediately prepare and submit the SEF Utilization Report for CY 2021 to the oversight agencies for monitoring, transparency and accountability in the allocation and utilization of the fund. We emphasized that timelines in the preparation and submission of the required reports cited in Section 6.1 of the above-cited circular must always be observed.
We recommended that Management require the LSB to ensure that the prescribed planning and budgeting tools are effectively utilized to attain optimal and timely SEF budget.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management require the concerned official to submit copies of perfected contracts and POs and their complete supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within five working days after execution or issuance, for auditorial review and technical evaluation by COA TAS.

We recommended that Management require the Accountant and the officials concerned to fill-up the relevant and required information on the POs and that the document must be signed and approved only when the required data are complete to ensure that the supplier will deliver the goods in accordance with the terms and conditions of the contract. Likewise, inform the Office of the Auditor within 24 hours upon acceptance of deliveries so that timely inspection will be made.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:
Strictly comply with the rules and regulations on fuel consumption to minimize wasteful, excessive and unnecessary expenditures through usage of requisition and issue voucher or its equivalent, and properly accomplished driver’s trip ticket in order to ensure that government vehicles are used reasonably and to avoid audit suspension or disallowance thereof;
Prepare and submit Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Fuel Consumption Report of LGU vehicles and heavy equipment to the Commission on Audit (thru Municipal Accountant) for verification and evaluation of the Auditor within 10 days of the ensuing month; and
Allocate a reasonable budget for fuel, oil and lubricant expenses and ensure that disbursements pertinent thereto are within the limits of the appropriation.

We recommended that Management:
Ensure that the approved AIP for 20 percent DF be submitted to DILG and DBM prior to implementation of the projects to facilitate evaluation thereof, if the same were intended for socio-economic development purposes;
Continue their progressive efforts in optimally utilizing the 20 percent DF in the implementation of development projects for the benefit of the constituents in the locality; and
Require the concerned personnel in charge of preparing the AIP to ensure that it is in accordance with the prescribed format showing the implementing office and the schedule of implementation per PPA.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Legislative body to enact immediately appropriation ordinance approving supplemental budget for the excess revenue generated by the LGU. The additional revenue can be used to finance other programs and activities of the LGU;
Ensure that the LFC perform its functions during budget preparation phase to attain reliable estimates of income to be used for the timely implementation of programs and activities; and
Exert more efforts in the collection of income from local sources.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure submission of paid DVs on or before the 10th day of the ensuing month, and avoid voluminous submissions of DVs pertaining to transactions for several months all at once;
Evaluate the process and exercise caution in the preparation of the DVs to avoid cancellation thereof and to prevent further occurrence of the noted deficiency in the succeeding years. Moreover, processing of vouchers must only be undertaken when they were properly approved and with complete documentation; and
Remind the concerned officials and employees of the administrative or criminal liability, under Section 39 (1) (3) of PD No. 1445, Section 23 (1) (3) of the Administrative Code of 1987 and Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code, which may be imposed as a consequence of their inability or refusal to render accounts without justifiable cause.

We recommend that the Bids and Awards Committee require the
submission of the documentations listed in Section 9.1.3.1 of COA Circular No.
2012-001 as part of the technical documents during the submission of bids.

We recommend that the end user who prepared the purchase request
send a justification on the basis used on the prices of items. Henceforth,
management is encouraged to abide by the procurement rules and regulations
of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 to prevent irregular transactions subject to
audit suspension and disallowance.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to submit explanation on the delayed submission of records and reports.  Henceforth, require them to cooperate and coordinate in the timely submission of require reports.  Otherwise, sanctions provided in Section 122 of P. D. 1445 or the State Audit Code of the Philippines could be imposed which is the suspension of payment of their salaries until they shall have complied with the requirements of the Commission on report submission.
